skills.prompt: This topic covers intermolecular forces for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-5.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.1). Students should understand that intermolecular forces (IMFs) are attractions between molecules, much weaker than intramolecular (covalent/ionic) bonds. Types ranked by strength: London dispersion forces (LDF, present in all molecules, increase with molar mass and surface area), dipole-dipole forces (between polar molecules), and hydrogen bonding (strong dipole-dipole between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on N, O, or F). Ion-dipole forces occur between ions and polar molecules in solution. Students must identify the strongest IMF present in a substance and use IMFs to explain physical properties (boiling point, vapor pressure, viscosity, surface tension). Connect to phase changes, solubility, and solution formation. AP exam note: predicting and comparing physical properties using IMFs is a core skill. Vocabulary: London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding, ion-dipole, intermolecular force, vapor pressure, boiling point.

This topic covers properties of solids for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-5.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.2). Students should understand the four types of crystalline solids and their properties. Ionic solids: high melting points, brittle, conduct electricity when dissolved. Molecular solids: low melting points, soft, poor conductors, held by IMFs. Covalent network solids (diamond, SiO2, SiC): very high melting points, very hard, poor conductors (except graphite). Metallic solids: variable melting points, malleable, good conductors. Amorphous solids lack long-range order. Students must classify solids by type and predict properties based on the dominant interparticle forces. Connect to bonding, IMFs, and material properties. AP exam note: classifying and comparing solid types based on properties is commonly tested. Vocabulary: ionic solid, molecular solid, covalent network solid, metallic solid, amorphous, crystalline, lattice, melting point.

This topic covers solids, liquids, and gases for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-6.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.3). Students should understand how particle behavior differs across phases: solids have fixed shape and volume with particles vibrating in place; liquids have fixed volume but variable shape with particles sliding past each other; gases have variable shape and volume with particles in rapid random motion. Phase changes (melting, boiling, sublimation, deposition, condensation, freezing) involve energy changes without temperature change at transition points. Heating curves show temperature vs. energy added, with plateaus at phase transitions. Students must interpret heating curves and relate phase behavior to intermolecular forces. Connect to IMFs, thermochemistry, and kinetic molecular theory. AP exam note: heating curve analysis and relating phase properties to IMF strength are tested. Vocabulary: phase, phase change, heating curve, enthalpy of fusion, enthalpy of vaporization, sublimation, vapor pressure.

This topic covers the ideal gas law for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.4). Students should understand that PV = nRT relates pressure (P, atm), volume (V, L), moles (n), the ideal gas constant (R = 0.0821 L-atm/mol-K), and temperature (T, Kelvin). The ideal gas law assumes particles have negligible volume and no intermolecular forces. Students must solve for any variable, convert units appropriately (especially temperature to Kelvin), and apply the combined gas law for changing conditions. At STP (0 degrees C, 1 atm), one mole of ideal gas occupies 22.4 L. Dalton's law of partial pressures states that total pressure equals the sum of partial pressures. Students must calculate molar mass and density of gases. Connect to stoichiometry, kinetic molecular theory, and real gas behavior. AP exam note: ideal gas law calculations appear frequently. Vocabulary: ideal gas law, STP, partial pressure, Dalton's law, molar volume, gas constant.

This topic covers kinetic molecular theory (KMT) for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.5). Students should understand the postulates of KMT: gas particles are in constant random motion, collisions are perfectly elastic, particle volume is negligible compared to container volume, there are no intermolecular forces, and average kinetic energy is proportional to absolute temperature (KE_avg = 3/2 kT). At the same temperature, lighter gases have higher average speeds (KE = 1/2 mv^2). Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution curves show the range of molecular speeds at different temperatures. Students must use KMT to explain gas behavior (pressure, temperature, effusion) and interpret distribution curves. Connect to ideal gas law, diffusion, effusion, and Graham's law. AP exam note: interpreting Maxwell-Boltzmann distributions is a key skill. Vocabulary: kinetic molecular theory, elastic collision, average kinetic energy, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, root mean square speed, effusion, diffusion.

This topic covers deviations from ideal gas behavior for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-7.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.6). Students should understand that real gases deviate from ideal behavior at high pressures (particles closer together, volume becomes significant) and low temperatures (particles move slowly, IMFs become significant). The van der Waals equation (P + a(n/V)^2)(V - nb) = nRT corrects for IMFs (a) and particle volume (b). Gases with stronger IMFs and larger particle sizes deviate more. At high temperatures and low pressures, real gases behave most ideally. Students must predict when and why deviations occur and identify which gases deviate most. Connect to IMFs, molecular size, and conditions of gas behavior. AP exam note: explaining conditions for ideal vs. real gas behavior is commonly tested. Vocabulary: real gas, ideal gas, van der Waals equation, compressibility factor, intermolecular forces, particle volume, deviation.

This topic covers solutions and mixtures for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.7). Students should understand that solutions are homogeneous mixtures with a solute dissolved in a solvent. Concentration can be expressed as molarity (M = mol solute / L solution), mass percent, mole fraction, or molality. Dilution follows M1V1 = M2V2. The dissolution process involves solute-solute, solvent-solvent, and solute-solvent interactions: dissolution is favored when solute-solvent interactions are comparable to or stronger than the original interactions (like dissolves like). Students must perform concentration calculations, dilution problems, and predict solubility based on polarity. Connect to colligative properties, reaction stoichiometry in solution, and intermolecular forces. AP exam note: molarity calculations and dilution problems are foundational. Vocabulary: solution, solute, solvent, molarity, dilution, concentration, homogeneous mixture, dissolution.

This topic covers representations of solutions for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.8). Students should understand how to represent solutions at the particulate level using diagrams that show solute particles (molecules or ions) distributed among solvent molecules. For ionic compounds, dissolution produces separated hydrated ions (not ion pairs or formula units). For molecular solutes, intact molecules are dispersed. Strong electrolytes fully dissociate (strong acids, strong bases, soluble salts); weak electrolytes partially dissociate; nonelectrolytes do not dissociate. Students must draw and interpret particulate diagrams of solutions and determine the species present. Connect to electrolyte behavior, conductivity, colligative properties, and net ionic equations. AP exam note: particulate diagram interpretation is a distinctive AP Chemistry skill. Vocabulary: electrolyte, nonelectrolyte, dissociation, hydration, ion-dipole interaction, particulate diagram, strong electrolyte, weak electrolyte.

This topic covers separation of solutions and mixtures for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.9). Students should understand that mixtures can be separated based on differences in physical properties of their components. Techniques include distillation (separates by boiling point differences), filtration (separates solids from liquids), chromatography (separates by differential affinity for stationary and mobile phases), and evaporation or crystallization (removes solvent to recover solute). Choosing the appropriate technique depends on the components' properties: volatility, solubility, particle size, and polarity. Students must select and justify separation techniques for given mixtures. Connect to solution properties, intermolecular forces, and laboratory skills. AP exam note: questions may describe a mixture and ask which separation method is appropriate. Vocabulary: distillation, filtration, chromatography, evaporation, crystallization, stationary phase, mobile phase, volatile.

This topic covers solubility for AP Chemistry students (CED: SPQ-3.D, Unit 3, Topic 3.10). Students should understand that solubility depends on intermolecular interactions between solute and solvent (like dissolves like): polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents, nonpolar in nonpolar. Temperature generally increases solubility of solids in liquids but decreases solubility of gases in liquids (Henry's law). Pressure affects gas solubility (Henry's law: S = kH * P). Saturated solutions are at equilibrium with undissolved solute; unsaturated can dissolve more; supersaturated contain more than equilibrium amount. Students must predict relative solubilities and explain using IMFs and thermodynamic considerations. Connect to solution equilibria, precipitation reactions, and Ksp. AP exam note: predicting and explaining solubility trends is tested. Vocabulary: solubility, saturated, unsaturated, supersaturated, Henry's law, miscible, immiscible, like dissolves like.

This topic covers spectroscopy and the electromagnetic spectrum for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.A, Unit 3, Topic 3.11). Students should understand that electromagnetic radiation has both wave and particle properties. The spectrum ranges from radio waves (low energy, long wavelength) to gamma rays (high energy, short wavelength). Key relationships: c = wavelength times frequency, and E = h times frequency (Planck's equation). Different regions of the EM spectrum interact with matter differently: UV-Vis causes electronic transitions, IR causes molecular vibrations, and microwave causes molecular rotations. Spectroscopy identifies substances by their characteristic absorption or emission patterns. Students must relate wavelength, frequency, and energy, and explain how spectroscopy provides structural information. Connect to atomic emission, Beer-Lambert law, and quantum mechanics. AP exam note: energy-frequency-wavelength calculations are foundational. Vocabulary: electromagnetic spectrum, wavelength, frequency, photon, Planck's constant, absorption, emission, spectroscopy.

This topic covers properties of photons for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.B, Unit 3, Topic 3.12). Students should understand that light behaves as both a wave and a particle (wave-particle duality). Photons are discrete packets of electromagnetic energy with energy E = hf = hc/wavelength, where h is Planck's constant. When atoms absorb photons, electrons transition to higher energy levels; when they emit photons, electrons drop to lower levels. The energy of the photon exactly equals the difference between energy levels. This explains atomic emission spectra (bright lines) and absorption spectra (dark lines). Students must calculate photon energy and relate it to electronic transitions. Connect to electron configuration, atomic spectra, and the Bohr model. AP exam note: photon energy calculations and connecting them to electronic transitions are key skills. Vocabulary: photon, wave-particle duality, quantized, energy level, electronic transition, emission spectrum, absorption spectrum, Planck's constant.

This topic covers the Beer-Lambert law for AP Chemistry students (CED: SAP-8.C, Unit 3, Topic 3.13). Students should understand that Beer's law relates the absorbance (A) of a solution to its concentration: A = epsilon times b times c, where epsilon is the molar absorptivity, b is the path length, and c is the concentration. Absorbance is proportional to concentration for dilute solutions. A calibration curve (absorbance vs. concentration plot) allows determination of unknown concentrations by interpolation. Transmittance (T) relates to absorbance: A = -log(T). Colorimetry and spectrophotometry use this principle for quantitative analysis. Students must construct and interpret calibration curves and calculate unknown concentrations. Connect to solution properties, spectroscopy, and laboratory techniques. AP exam note: Beer's law calculations and calibration curve interpretation are commonly tested. Vocabulary: absorbance, transmittance, molar absorptivity, path length, calibration curve, Beer-Lambert law, spectrophotometer.
